name: Implement Rate Limiting with Redis
description: Build an API rate limiter using Redis counters and sliding windows to protect services from abuse and traffic spikes.
trendingReason: Essential for API security and preventing abuse in production services

steps.title: Implement fixed-window rate limiting
steps.description: Use INCR with EXPIRE to count requests per time window.
steps.command:
redis-cli MULTI
redis-cli INCR ratelimit:user:1001:$(date +%s -d '1 minute ago' | cut -c1-9)0
redis-cli EXPIRE ratelimit:user:1001:$(date +%s | cut -c1-9)0 120
redis-cli EXEC
steps.tip: The key includes a truncated timestamp so it auto-rotates each window.

steps.title: Simple counter increment and check
steps.description: Increment a counter for the current minute and check against a limit.
steps.command: redis-cli SET ratelimit:api:user1001 0 EX 60 NX && redis-cli INCR ratelimit:api:user1001
steps.tip: NX ensures the key is only set if it does not exist, preserving the counter within the window.

steps.title: Check current request count
steps.description: Read the current counter value to decide if the request should be allowed.

steps.title: Implement sliding window with sorted sets
steps.description: Use a sorted set to track individual request timestamps for a precise sliding window.
steps.command: redis-cli ZADD ratelimit:sliding:user1001 $(date +%s) $(uuidgen) && redis-cli ZREMRANGEBYSCORE ratelimit:sliding:user1001 0 $(($(date +%s)-60)) && redis-cli ZCARD ratelimit:sliding:user1001
steps.warning: Sorted set rate limiting uses more memory than counters. Use it only when precision matters.

steps.title: Set expiration on sliding window key
steps.description: Ensure the sorted set key expires to prevent memory leaks for inactive users.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Redis Rate Limiting Demo
set -euo pipefail

USER_ID="user1001"
LIMIT=10
WINDOW=60

echo "=== Fixed Window Rate Limiter ==="
KEY="ratelimit:api:$USER_ID"
redis-cli SET "$KEY" 0 EX $WINDOW NX > /dev/null 2>&1
COUNT=$(redis-cli INCR "$KEY")
echo "Request count: $COUNT / $LIMIT"

if [ "$COUNT" -gt "$LIMIT" ]; then
  echo "RATE LIMITED - request denied"
  TTL=$(redis-cli TTL "$KEY")
  echo "Retry after: $TTL seconds"
else
  echo "ALLOWED - request processed"
fi

echo ""
echo "=== Sliding Window Rate Limiter ==="
SKEY="ratelimit:sliding:$USER_ID"
NOW=$(date +%s)
WINDOW_START=$((NOW - WINDOW))

# Remove old entries
redis-cli ZREMRANGEBYSCORE "$SKEY" 0 "$WINDOW_START" > /dev/null
# Add current request
redis-cli ZADD "$SKEY" "$NOW" "$NOW:$$" > /dev/null
redis-cli EXPIRE "$SKEY" $((WINDOW * 2)) > /dev/null
# Check count
SCOUNT=$(redis-cli ZCARD "$SKEY")
echo "Sliding window count: $SCOUNT / $LIMIT"

if [ "$SCOUNT" -gt "$LIMIT" ]; then
  echo "RATE LIMITED - request denied"
else
  echo "ALLOWED - request processed"
fi

faq.question: Fixed window vs sliding window rate limiting?
faq.answer: Fixed window is simpler and uses less memory (one counter per window) but allows burst traffic at window boundaries. Sliding window is precise but uses a sorted set entry per request.

faq.question: How do I return rate limit headers to the client?
faq.answer: After checking the counter, return X-RateLimit-Limit (max), X-RateLimit-Remaining (limit - count), and X-RateLimit-Reset (TTL of the key as Unix timestamp) in your HTTP response headers.

faq.question: Will rate limiting work across multiple app servers?
faq.answer: Yes, since all servers share the same Redis instance, the counters are centralized and consistent across all application nodes.
